scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
justification: Meets most pass criteria (7 rules extracted, 5+ escalations as clarifying questions, 'demand' explicitly flagged as undefined, confidence at 30, no specific values presented as requirements). However, critically violates the 'No Given/When/Then criteria written' pass criterion: the output includes 6 full acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format (AC-1 through AC-6) AND 7 testable_assertions in GIVEN/WHEN/THEN format embedded in each business rule. Writing GWT criteria from this level of vagueness is explicitly listed as a failure mode. The recommendations in escalations suggest specific values (e.g., '50%-300% of base price', '1-5 minute rolling window') but these are clearly labeled as recommendations rather than requirements, so they don't constitute invented values presented as specs.

observations.text: Confidence level of 30 is appropriately low and well-justified — correctly identifies this as an executive vision statement, not a specification
Excellent ambiguity surfacing: 5 detailed escalations covering demand definition, guardrail specifics, competitive vs profitable tension, real-time SLA, and manual override — all critical gaps
Correctly flags CEO's 'probably' on guardrails as 'deeply concerning' and elevates guardrails to invariant status regardless
7 business rules extracted with invariant classifications and detailed justifications, exceeding the 5-rule minimum
8 edge cases identified including subtle ones like price flicker from rapid oscillation and jurisdictional regulatory constraints
Wrote 6 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-6) despite the input being too vague to support them — this is the primary rubric violation
Each of the 7 business rules includes a testable_assertion in GIVEN/WHEN/THEN format, compounding the GWT violation
Business rules lack explicit individual confidence ratings (e.g., high/medium/low per rule) — only invariant flags are provided, missing the expected per-rule confidence assessment
The domain analysis is quite comprehensive (detailed stakeholder impact, edge cases, full ubiquitous language) which borders on treating the input as more specified than it actually is

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"CEO described a dynamic pricing system ('DynaPricing') for delivery services at an all-hands meeting. The system adjusts delivery prices based on real-time demand signals. The description is high-level and intentionally vague — typical of an executive vision statement, not a product specification.
